section.title: Driving Out the Nations
section.sectionRationale: This chapter contains the severe command to devote the Canaanite nations to destruction, language bound tightly to a specific, unrepeatable moment in redemptive history when Israel functioned as God's chosen instrument of judgment on centuries of accumulated wickedness; it must be read with interpretive honesty, never as a warrant for violence in any later era.
section.thesis: Because the LORD chose Israel not for any greatness of their own but purely out of love and covenant faithfulness, He commands them to devote the Canaanite nations and their idols to complete destruction, a description of a bounded moment in redemptive history that anticipates final judgment on all evil and the spiritual warfare of the new covenant, never a warrant for violence today.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the command to devote the nations to destruction; Israel chosen not for size but for love; the blessing that follows obedience; the call not to fear the nations, remembering Egypt; and the warning to burn the idols and refuse their silver and gold.

units.label: Devote them to complete destruction
units.note: make no treaty, show no mercy

verses.text: When the LORD your God brings you into the land that you are entering to possess, and He drives out before you many nations, the Hittites, Girgashites, Amorites, Canaanites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites, seven nations larger and stronger than you,
and when the LORD your God has delivered them over to you to defeat them, then you must {{cherem|devote}} them to complete destruction. Make no treaty with them and show them no mercy.
Do not intermarry with them. Do not give your daughters to their sons or take their daughters for your sons,
because they will turn your sons away from following Me to serve other gods. Then the anger of the LORD will burn against you, and He will swiftly destroy you.
Instead, this is what you are to do to them: tear down their altars, smash their sacred pillars, cut down their Asherah poles, and burn their idols in the fire.

units.label: A holy people, chosen not for size but for love

verses.text: For you are a people {{qadosh|holy}} to the LORD your God. The LORD your God has chosen you to be a people for His {{segullah|prized possession}} out of all peoples on the face of the earth.
The LORD did not set His affection on you and choose you because you were more numerous than the other peoples, for you were the fewest of all peoples.
But because the LORD loved you and kept the oath He swore to your fathers, He brought you out with a mighty hand and redeemed you from the house of slavery, from the hand of Pharaoh king of Egypt.
Know therefore that the LORD your God is God, the faithful God who keeps His covenant of {{chesed|loving devotion}} for a thousand generations of those who love Him and keep His commandments.
But those who hate Him He repays to their faces with destruction; He will not hesitate to repay to his face the one who hates Him.
So keep the commandments and statutes and ordinances that I am giving you to follow this day.
If you listen to these ordinances and keep them carefully, then the LORD your God will keep His covenant and the loving devotion that He swore to your fathers.

He will love you and bless you and multiply you. He will bless the fruit of your womb and the produce of your land, your grain, new wine, and oil, the young of your herds and the lambs of your flocks, in the land that He swore to your fathers to give you.
You will be blessed above all peoples; among you there will be no barren man or woman or livestock.
And the LORD will remove from you all sickness. He will not lay upon you any of the terrible diseases you knew in Egypt, but He will inflict them on all who hate you.
You must destroy all the peoples the LORD your God will deliver to you. Do not look on them with pity. Do not worship their gods, for that will be a snare to you.

units.label: Do not fear them: remember Egypt

verses.text: You may say in your heart, “These nations are greater than we are; how can we drive them out?”
But do not be afraid of them. Be sure to remember what the LORD your God did to Pharaoh and all Egypt:

the great trials that you saw, the signs and wonders, and the mighty hand and outstretched arm by which the LORD your God brought you out. The LORD your God will do the same to all the peoples you now fear.
Moreover, the LORD your God will send the hornet against them until even the survivors hiding from you have perished.
Do not be terrified by them, for the LORD your God, who is among you, is a great and awesome God.
The LORD your God will drive out these nations before you little by little. You will not be enabled to eliminate them all at once, or the wild animals would multiply around you.
But the LORD your God will give them over to you and throw them into great confusion, until they are destroyed.
He will hand their kings over to you, and you will wipe out their names from under heaven. No one will be able to stand against you; you will annihilate them.

units.label: Burn the idols; do not covet

verses.text: You must burn up the images of their gods; do not covet the silver and gold that is on them or take it for yourselves, or you will be ensnared by it; for it is detestable to the LORD your God.
And you must not bring any detestable thing into your house, or you, like it, will be set apart for destruction. You are to utterly detest and abhor it, because it is set apart for destruction.

cherem.gloss: To devote irrevocably to destruction, setting something apart entirely for the LORD by destroying it, so nothing of it may be kept for personal gain. It describes a specific, bounded act of divine judgment carried out through Israel at this stage of redemptive history, not a general rule of warfare.

qadosh.gloss: Holy, set apart. Israel's holiness here is a status given by God's choice, not earned by merit or size; it grounds the call to be different from the surrounding nations, especially in worship.

segullah.gloss: A treasured, personal possession, often used of a king's private treasure. Applied to Israel, it expresses God's intimate, valuing love for His people, later applied to the church, 1 Peter 2:9.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast covenant loyalty. Combines love and faithfulness in a single word, describing God's unwavering commitment to keep His covenant promises across generations, grounded in His own character rather than in Israel's merit.

basic.context: Moses commands Israel to devote the Canaanite nations and their idols to complete destruction as they enter the land, grounding this severe instruction not in Israel's own greatness but in God's covenant love and faithfulness to the promise He swore to their fathers.
basic.hermeneutics: This is one of the hardest passages in Deuteronomy, and it deserves honesty: it describes a specific, bounded act of divine judgment carried out through Israel at a unique moment in redemptive history, ending centuries of accumulated Canaanite wickedness, Genesis 15:16, not a timeless rule for how any nation or individual should treat others. The New Testament redirects God's people entirely away from national conquest toward spiritual warfare against sin and evil, and toward advancing Christ's kingdom by the gospel, not the sword.
basic.originalLanguages: Cherem, devote to destruction, describes an act set irrevocably apart for God, forbidding any personal profit from it. Israel is qadosh, holy, and segullah, a treasured possession, chosen purely by God's chesed, His loving, faithful devotion, not by their size or merit.
basic.typology: The devoted destruction of Canaanite idolatry anticipates the final, complete judgment of all evil at the end of the age, and Israel's calling as a treasured possession chosen by grace anticipates the church, gathered from every nation purely by God's mercy, 1 Peter 2:9-10.
basic.christ: This chapter's severity belongs to Israel's unique role as a national, political body carrying out God's specific judgment in that era; the New Testament redirects believers to spiritual warfare, not against flesh and blood, but against evil itself, Ephesians 6:12, since Christ's kingdom advances by gospel proclamation, not by the sword, John 18:36. The certain final judgment on all evil, which this chapter foreshadows in miniature, is entrusted entirely to Christ, the righteous judge.
basic.god: God is holy and will not tolerate idolatry, yet the driving motive behind this whole chapter is His covenant love, chesed, freely set on Israel not because of their merit but because He loved them and kept His ancient oath, showing that even His severest commands in this passage flow from faithful, covenant love rather than caprice.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 15:16 explains that Canaan's judgment awaited the completion of the Amorites' iniquity, showing this was a judgment on real accumulated wickedness, not ethnic hostility. Ephesians 6:12 redirects the church's warfare to spiritual powers, not human enemies. 1 Peter 2:9-10 applies Israel's identity as a treasured, holy people to the church.
basic.oneStory: This chapter names Israel's calling in stark, severe terms bound to a specific moment in redemptive history, while revealing that even here, God's motive is covenant love, not caprice. The New Testament fulfills its deeper pattern, final judgment on all evil and a people treasured purely by grace, in Christ, who conquers not with the sword but through the cross, and who gathers His treasured people from every nation by His Spirit.

context.literary: This chapter follows the Shema's call to exclusive love for God with the practical, severe implications of that exclusivity for Israel's relationship to the nations already occupying the land, before turning to the promised blessings of obedience.
context.historical: Genesis 15:16 already anticipated this moment centuries earlier, telling Abraham that his descendants would not possess the land until 'the iniquity of the Amorites' had reached its full measure, framing the conquest as judgment on entrenched, generations-long wickedness rather than an act of ethnic conquest for its own sake.
context.canonical: This passage sits at the heart of the church's ongoing, honest wrestling with the conquest narratives; it is never cited in the New Testament as a pattern for believers to imitate, and the New Testament consistently redirects God's people toward spiritual warfare and gospel proclamation rather than physical conquest.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To prevent Israel's assimilation into Canaanite idolatry by commanding a complete and decisive break from it, rooting the severity of that command in God's own holiness, in Israel's calling as a treasured, set-apart people, and in His covenant love and faithfulness rather than in Israel's own merit.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific, unrepeatable act of divine judgment carried out through Israel as a national, political body at a unique stage of redemptive history; it is not prescriptive for any nation, group, or individual today. What is prescriptive and abiding is the call to decisive, uncompromising separation from idolatry in one's own life and worship, and trust that final judgment on all evil belongs to God alone.

debates.question: How should Christians read the command to 'devote to complete destruction' and 'show no mercy' toward the Canaanite nations?

views.label: Bounded, unrepeatable old-covenant judgment
views.case: This command belongs to a unique moment in redemptive history, when Israel functioned as a nation-state carrying out God's specific judgment on centuries of Canaanite wickedness, Genesis 15:16, comparable to a divinely appointed act of judgment rather than a general ethic of warfare; no such national mandate exists after the coming of Christ, whose kingdom advances by gospel proclamation, not conquest, John 18:36.

views.label: A morally difficult text requiring honest, humble wrestling
views.case: Some interpreters stress that the totality of the command, including women and children, remains a genuinely hard question that should be faced honestly rather than resolved too neatly or too quickly, while still affirming that it cannot be treated as a model for any nation or individual today.

debates.bearing: Both views agree this text must never be used to justify violence against any people today, and that in the new covenant the LORD's people wage war against sin and spiritual evil, not against human nations, Ephesians 6:12, 2 Corinthians 10:3-4.

study.originalLanguages: Cherem describes an irrevocable devoting to destruction, forbidding any personal profit from what is judged; Israel itself is qadosh, holy, and God's segullah, treasured possession, chosen purely on the basis of chesed, His loving, covenant-keeping devotion, not their size or worth.

typology.type: The devoted destruction of Canaanite idolatry
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates the final, complete judgment of all evil at the end of the age, entrusted entirely to Christ the righteous judge, Revelation 19:11-16.

typology.type: Israel chosen as a treasured possession purely by grace
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the church, a people gathered from every nation by grace alone to be God's own treasured possession, 1 Peter 2:9-10.

study.christ: The severe language of this chapter belongs to Israel's unique calling as a national, political body carrying out a specific act of God's judgment on entrenched wickedness at one moment in redemptive history; it is never repeated as instruction for the church. Christ's kingdom instead advances through the proclamation of the gospel, not the sword, John 18:36, and the church's warfare is against spiritual powers of evil, not human enemies, Ephesians 6:12. The certainty of final judgment on all evil, which this chapter foreshadows in miniature, is entrusted entirely to Christ, who will judge with perfect righteousness at the end of the age.
study.god: God is holy, tolerating no rival worship, and He is also faithful in chesed, covenant love, choosing Israel not for their size or merit but purely because He loved them and kept His ancient promise. Even in a chapter this severe, His governing motive is revealed to be steadfast love and covenant faithfulness, not caprice or ethnic preference.

crossReferences.note: Explains that the conquest awaited the completion of the Amorites' iniquity, framing it as judgment on real, accumulated wickedness.
Redirects the church's warfare entirely to spiritual powers of evil, not human enemies or nations.
Christ declares His kingdom is not of this world and does not advance by armed force.
Applies Israel's identity as a holy, treasured people to the church, gathered by mercy alone.

study.oneStory: This chapter names Israel's calling in stark and severe terms bound tightly to a specific, unrepeatable moment in redemptive history, while revealing that even here God's driving motive is covenant love, not caprice. Its deeper pattern, final judgment on all evil and a people treasured purely by grace, is fulfilled in Christ, who conquers not by the sword but through the cross, and who gathers His treasured people from every nation by His Spirit.

sermon.title: Grace, Therefore, Holy
sermon.why: Keller preaches directly on Deuteronomy 7:6-11, showing that Israel's calling to holiness and separation flows entirely from grace already received, not from merit, a crucial framework for reading this chapter's hardest commands honestly.

quiz.q: Why did the LORD choose Israel, according to verses 7-8?

options.text: Not because of their size or merit, but because He loved them and kept His oath to their fathers.
options.note: Verses 7-8 state this explicitly, ruling out size or merit as the reason.

options.text: Because they were the largest and strongest of all the nations.
options.note: Verse 7 explicitly says the opposite, that they were the fewest of all peoples.

options.text: Because Israel had already earned it through perfect obedience.
options.note: The text grounds the choice in God's love and oath, not in Israel's prior obedience.

options.text: No reason at all is given for God's choice.
options.note: Verses 7-8 give an explicit, twofold reason.

quiz.q: What does Moses instruct Israel to do to the altars, pillars, and idols of the nations (v.5)?

options.text: Tear them down, smash them, cut them down, and burn them in the fire.
options.note: Verse 5 lists these exact actions against every form of Canaanite worship object.

options.text: Preserve them carefully as historical artifacts.
options.note: The instruction is total destruction, the opposite of preservation.

options.text: Sell them to neighboring nations for silver and gold.
options.note: Verse 25 explicitly forbids taking silver or gold from these objects for personal gain.

options.text: Nothing is instructed regarding these objects.
options.note: Verse 5 gives explicit, detailed instructions.

quiz.q: What promise does Moses give regarding Israel's health and fertility if they obey (vv.12-15)?

options.text: The LORD will love, bless, and multiply them, and remove sickness so there is no barren man, woman, or animal among them.
options.note: Verses 13-15 describe these specific blessings of fruitfulness and health.

options.text: They would never need to work the land again.
options.note: The blessing described is abundance and fruitfulness through the land, not an absence of labor.

options.text: They would be exempt from all future warfare.
options.note: The chapter's larger context is precisely about warfare against the nations in the land, not exemption from it.

options.text: No promise regarding health or blessing is given.
options.note: Verses 12-15 give an extensive, specific promise.

quiz.q: According to verse 22, how would the LORD drive out the nations from the land?

options.text: Little by little, so that wild animals would not overrun the land too quickly.
options.note: Verse 22 gives this exact reasoning for a gradual conquest.

options.text: Instantly, in a single day.
options.note: The text specifically describes a gradual process, not an instant one.

options.text: Through a plague that Israel itself would carry.
options.note: No such mechanism is described; verse 20 mentions the LORD sending the hornet, not a plague carried by Israel.

options.text: The text says the nations would never actually be driven out.
options.note: Verses 22-24 describe their eventual, complete removal, only gradually rather than all at once.

quiz.q: What warning does Moses give about silver and gold found on idols (v.25)?

options.text: Not to covet or take it, since it is detestable and would ensnare them.
options.note: Verse 25 gives this explicit warning against taking idols' precious metals.

options.text: To melt it down and give it entirely to the priests.
options.note: No such instruction is given; the command is to avoid taking it at all.

options.text: To keep it as spoils of war for personal wealth.
options.note: The verse explicitly forbids this, calling it a snare and something detestable.

options.text: No warning is given about idols' silver and gold.
options.note: Verse 25 gives an explicit and specific warning.

questions: Israel was chosen not because of size or merit but purely because God loved them. Where do you still tie your worth or belonging to God to your own performance rather than to His love?

This chapter's command belongs to a specific, bounded moment in redemptive history; the New Testament redirects God's people to spiritual warfare against sin and evil, never violence against people. What idols in your own heart need to be torn down and burned rather than quietly tolerated?
Moses warns Israel not to be ensnared by the silver and gold on Canaanite idols. Where might something outwardly valuable actually be entangling you with something spiritually harmful?
God said He would drive out the nations 'little by little.' Where do you need patience with a slow, gradual work of God in your life rather than expecting instant transformation?
Israel is called 'a people holy to the LORD, His treasured possession' purely by grace. How does knowing you are treasured, rather than merely tolerated, by God change how you see yourself today?

translationNotes.note: 'Devote to complete destruction' translates cherem, an irrevocable setting-apart for judgment; Genesis 15:16 frames this as judgment on the Amorites' accumulated iniquity, a bounded moment in redemptive history rather than a timeless warfare ethic.
'Treasured possession' translates segullah, a term used elsewhere for a king's personal treasure, expressing God's intimate valuing of Israel rather than mere political usefulness.

'Covenant of loving devotion' renders chesed, a word combining steadfast love and faithfulness, describing God's unwavering commitment to His promises across generations, grounded in His own character rather than Israel's merit.
